metadata.title: The Medical Prompt: Four Guardrails That Can't Be Missing
sources: Anthropic Courses — Real World Prompting, Lesson 2: Medical Prompt (github.com/anthropics/courses/real_world_prompting)

beats.narration_text: The claim that a high-stakes medical prompt has four guardrails that cannot be missing really landed for me, because in that domain a confident wrong answer is dangerous. Help me build one properly: (1) name what those four guardrails likely are — scope limits, uncertainty flags, source grounding, and a defer-to-clinician clause; (2) write a prompt for a symptom-checker assistant that bakes all four in; (3) show me one adversarial user question that would slip past a prompt missing the uncertainty guardrail.

props.command: The claim that a high-stakes medical prompt has four guardrails that cannot be missing really landed for me, because in that domain a confident wrong answer is dangerous. Help me build one properly: (1) name what those four guardrails likely are — scope limits, uncertainty flags, source grounding, and a defer-to-clinician clause; (2) write a prompt for a symptom-checker assistant that bakes all four in; (3) show me one adversarial user question that would slip past a prompt missing the uncertainty guardrail.
output: Four required elements: role, uncertainty, escalation, disclaimer
Missing any one → confident answers to dangerous questions
Bad prompt: 'Can you summarize this patient record?'
Consequences here are real, not just bad output quality

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The bad prompt is a real example from the course: 'I have this patient medical record. Can you summarize it for me? I need this for a quick review before the patient's appointment tomorrow.' Claude generates a summary — but it's inconsistent across patients, no structured sections, no escalation language, and it will confidently answer follow-up questions that should be deflected to a physician. That's the failure state before any guardrails are added.

props.artifactHeading: No role. No uncertainty. No escalation. No disclaimer.
artifactLines: 'I have this patient record. Can you summarize it for me?'
Output: inconsistent format across patients
No safety language, no 'see a doctor' triggers
Will answer follow-up diagnostic questions confidently
This is the real starting point from Anthropic's course

beats.narration_text: Guardrail one: role definition. The system prompt establishes Claude as a highly experienced medical professional specializing in concise, actionable summaries. That framing changes how Claude weights information — it filters for clinically relevant facts rather than everything in the record. Guardrail two: structured output. The prompt specifies exactly which sections appear and in which order — diagnoses, medications, other treatments, recent concerns, action items.

props.artifactHeading: Role + Structure: who Claude is and what it produces.
artifactLines: Role: 'highly experienced medical professional — concise summaries'
Effect: weights clinical relevance over completeness
Structure: Key Diagnoses → Medications → Treatments → Concerns → Actions
Wrap input in <patient_record> tags to isolate data from instructions
Wrap output in <summary> tags for programmatic extraction

beats.narration_text: Guardrail three: uncertainty language. The course calls this the 'language ladder' — from 'possible' to 'likely' to 'you should see a doctor.' The prompt must specify when each level applies. Guardrail four: the do-not-diagnose constraint. The prompt distinguishes between summarizing a record — which is allowed — and generating new diagnoses, which is not. A prompt that doesn't draw that line will have Claude diagnosing patients from summaries.

artifactLines: Language ladder: 'possible' → 'likely' → 'consult your physician'
Escalation trigger: emergency symptoms → redirect to emergency services
Do-not-diagnose: summarize existing record, never generate new diagnoses
Example action item: 'Monitor kidney function' not 'Patient has early CKD'
Without this line Claude will diagnose from summaries — confidently

beats.narration_text: The result when all four guardrails are in place: every patient record produces a structured summary with the same sections in the same order — extractable by code, readable by a physician in 30 seconds. The course goes one step further and shows how to convert the structured text output to JSON, which makes the action items queryable across a full day's patient list. Same underlying prompt logic — just different output format.

artifactLines: Every record: same sections, same order, same safety language
Output inside <summary> tags — extractable by regex or parser
JSON variant: action_items array queryable across all patients
Example use: generate daily action items list for physician team
Same prompt logic — format switch from text to JSON is one change

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Write a system prompt for a medical record summarizer. It must include: a role definition that weights clinical relevance, an uncertainty language ladder, an escalation trigger for emergency symptoms, and a strict do-not-diagnose constraint. Output each summary in XML summary tags with five fixed sections.' This prompt will show you exactly how Claude handles role framing under constraint — whether the guardrails actually change its output or just get acknowledged and ignored. Test it on a real case. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Write a system prompt for a medical record summarizer. Must include: role definition weighting clinical relevance, uncertainty language ladder, escalation trigger for emergencies, strict do-not-diagnose constraint. Output inside <summary> XML tags with five fixed sections.
props.segment: The Medical Prompt: Four Guardrails That Can't Be Missing
output: Claude writes a structured medical system prompt
Guardrails reveal how role framing changes output behavior
Test against a real patient record to verify each constraint

beats.narration_text: The Medical Prompt: Four Guardrails That Can't Be Missing
